title: International capital flows
subtitle: Unit 18 'The nation and the world economy’ Section 18.2 'Globalization and investment' in The CORE Team, The Economy. Available at https://tinyco.re/18020835 [Figure 18.8]
note: Data points show the mid-year of the 5-year average absolute current account balances as a % of GDP. CC-BY-ND-NC
caption:
International capital flows

Unit 18 'The nation and the world economy’ Section 18.2 'Globalization and investment' in The CORE Team, The Economy. Available at https://tinyco.re/18020835 [Figure 18.8]

This line chart tracks international capital flows from 1872 to 2017, with the vertical axis measuring the metric as a percentage of GDP ranging from 0% to 7%. The data points represent the mid-year of a 5-year average of absolute current account balances. The timeline begins in 1872 with a value near 3.7%, followed by a rise to a local peak slightly above 5% around 1890. After a subsequent decline, the line ascends again to reach the chart's absolute maximum in 1920, where the value exceeds 6.5%. Following this high point, the trend drops sharply, falling below 2.5% by 1930 and reaching a low point near 1.5% around 1940. A brief recovery occurs in the 1940s, peaking near 3.7%, before the line descends to its lowest levels in the mid-20th century. Between 1960 and 1970, the values hover between 1.3% and 1.5%. From the 1970s onward, the line shows a general upward trajectory, crossing the 2% mark in the late 1970s and rising steadily through the 1990s. A second major peak occurs around 2007, reaching nearly 5%, before declining to approximately 4% by the final data point in 2017.
